title: Executive Order 12114—Environmental Effects Abroad of Major Federal Actions

flags.justification: Distributed across every Federal agency taking covered actions, with the Department of State and Council on Environmental Quality in coordinating, not controlling, roles.
A specific eight-month implementation deadline and required consultation with State and CEQ on agency procedures.

Multiple categories of covered actions (global commons, foreign nations, toxic exports, protected resources) all coherently serve one purpose: extending environmental review to extraterritorial effects.

Sec. 2-5(b) allows agencies to modify the content, timing, and availability of environmental review documents based on a long list of foreign-policy, national-security, and commercial factors, with no defined limits on how broadly these grounds may be invoked.
The breadth of self-determined modification grounds (Flag 4) creates a structural incentive for agencies to invoke them to minimize environmental review burden.
A standing environmental-policy framework, evergreen by nature.

Sec. 3-1 explicitly states 'nothing in this Order shall be construed to create a cause of action' — an explicit foreclosure of judicial enforcement of the Order's own procedural requirements by affected parties.

Combined with the no-cause-of-action provision (Flag 7) and the broad, agency-self-determined exemption and modification authority (Flags 4-5 and 11), no meaningful external check exists on how agencies exercise this authority.

A genuinely novel extension of NEPA-style environmental review to actions with extraterritorial effects — this order remains the operative framework for such review to this day, a significant and lasting institutional innovation.
Sec. 3-3 designates a lead agency for multi-agency actions, a sensible, designed coordination mechanism.

Sec. 2-5(a) lists seven broad categorical exemptions, including one (actions the agency itself determines lack significant effect) that is entirely self-judging with no external check — particularly significant in combination with the no-cause-of-action provision (Flag 7), since no party can challenge an agency's own 'not significant' determination.

notes: Validation batch (positive). Structural weight: 6/22 = 27.3%. A significant, still-operative extension of environmental review to actions affecting the environment abroad, but with real structural concerns — the combination of a no-cause-of-action clause with broad, self-determined exemption categories is a notable design pattern worth watching for elsewhere in the corpus.
